guidelines.title: ACLS Cardiac Arrest and Resuscitation Protocols
guidelines.content:
ACLS CARDIAC ARREST AND RESUSCITATION PROTOCOLS

CARDIAC ARREST - SHOCKABLE RHYTHMS (VF/pVT):

Defibrillation:
- Biphasic: 120-200 J (manufacturer-specific) initial, same or higher subsequent
- Monophasic: 360 J all shocks
- Shock immediately when rhythm identified
- Resume CPR immediately after shock for 2 minutes

Medications:
- Epinephrine 1 mg IV/IO every 3-5 minutes
- Amiodarone:
  - First dose: 300 mg IV/IO bolus
  - Second dose: 150 mg IV/IO
- Alternative: Lidocaine 1-1.5 mg/kg first dose, then 0.5-0.75 mg/kg

Sequence:
1. CPR while preparing defibrillator
2. Shock when ready
3. Resume CPR x2 minutes
4. Check rhythm - if shockable, shock again
5. Epinephrine after second shock
6. Amiodarone after third shock
7. Continue cycle

CARDIAC ARREST - NON-SHOCKABLE (Asystole/PEA):

Medications:
- Epinephrine 1 mg IV/IO every 3-5 minutes
- NO defibrillation (check electrodes, leads)
- NO amiodarone/lidocaine

Reversible Causes (H's and T's):
- Hypovolemia: Fluids
- Hypoxia: Airway, ventilation
- Hydrogen ion (acidosis): Sodium bicarbonate if severe
- Hypo/Hyperkalemia: Calcium, insulin/glucose, bicarbonate
- Hypothermia: Warm fluids, active rewarming
- Tension pneumothorax: Needle decompression
- Tamponade: Pericardiocentesis
- Toxins: Specific antidotes
- Thrombosis (coronary): Consider PCI if ROSC
- Thrombosis (pulmonary): Consider thrombolytics

POST-CARDIAC ARREST CARE:

Targeted Temperature Management:
- Target: 32-36°C for 24 hours
- Cooling methods: IV cold saline, cooling blankets, intravascular devices
- Avoid fever (>37.5°C) for 72 hours

Hemodynamics:
- Target MAP >65 mmHg
- SBP >90 mmHg
- Vasopressors: Norepinephrine first-line
- Consider early coronary angiography for STEMI

SYMPTOMATIC BRADYCARDIA:

Atropine:
- 1 mg IV every 3-5 minutes
- Maximum total: 3 mg
- May be ineffective for infranodal blocks

Transcutaneous Pacing:
- If atropine ineffective
- Start at 60 mA, increase until capture
- Rate 60-80 bpm

Dopamine Infusion:
- 5-20 mcg/kg/min
- Alternative if pacing not available

Epinephrine Infusion:
- 2-10 mcg/min
- Alternative to dopamine

UNSTABLE TACHYCARDIA:

Synchronized Cardioversion:
- Narrow regular (SVT): 50-100 J
- Narrow irregular (AFib): 120-200 J biphasic
- Wide regular (VT): 100 J
- Wide irregular: Defibrillation dose (NOT synchronized)

STABLE TACHYCARDIA:

Narrow Complex Regular (SVT):
- Vagal maneuvers (modified Valsalva, carotid massage)
- Adenosine:
  - First dose: 6 mg rapid IV push + flush
  - Second dose: 12 mg if needed
  - Third dose: 12 mg if needed
- If adenosine fails: Diltiazem 15-20 mg IV or beta-blocker

Narrow Complex Irregular (AFib with RVR):
- Diltiazem: 0.25 mg/kg IV over 2 min, may repeat 0.35 mg/kg
- Metoprolol: 2.5-5 mg IV over 2 min, repeat q5min (max 15 mg)
- Amiodarone if HFrEF: 150 mg over 10 min

Wide Complex Regular (VT or uncertain):
- Amiodarone: 150 mg IV over 10 min, may repeat
- Procainamide: 20-50 mg/min until arrhythmia suppressed (max 17 mg/kg)
- If VT with pulse converts to VF: Defibrillate

MEDICATION SUMMARY:

Epinephrine:
- Cardiac arrest: 1 mg IV/IO q3-5min
- Anaphylaxis: 0.3-0.5 mg IM (1:1000)
- Infusion: 1-10 mcg/min

Amiodarone:
- VF/pVT arrest: 300 mg bolus, then 150 mg
- Stable VT: 150 mg over 10 min
- Post-arrest infusion: 1 mg/min x6h, then 0.5 mg/min x18h

Adenosine:
- SVT: 6 mg rapid push, then 12 mg x2
- Give via large peripheral or central vein
- Immediately flush with 20 mL saline

Calcium Chloride:
- Hyperkalemia: 1-2 g (10-20 mL of 10%) IV over 2-5 min
- Calcium channel blocker toxicity: 1-2 g IV

Sodium Bicarbonate:
- Hyperkalemia, TCA overdose: 1-2 mEq/kg IV
- NOT routine in cardiac arrest

Magnesium Sulfate:
- Torsades de pointes: 1-2 g IV over 5-20 min
- Hypomagnesemia: 1-2 g IV

EVIDENCE LEVEL: Level I (AHA ACLS Guidelines 2020)

guidelines.title: Anaphylaxis and Allergic Emergency Treatment
guidelines.content:
ANAPHYLAXIS TREATMENT PROTOCOL

EPINEPHRINE (FIRST-LINE - Give Immediately):

Intramuscular (Preferred):
- Adults: 0.3-0.5 mg (0.3-0.5 mL of 1:1000) IM in lateral thigh
- Pediatric: 0.01 mg/kg (max 0.3 mg for <30 kg, 0.5 mg for >30 kg)
- May repeat every 5-15 minutes x2-3 doses if needed
- Auto-injector doses:
  - EpiPen Jr: 0.15 mg (15-30 kg)
  - EpiPen: 0.3 mg (>30 kg)

Intravenous (Refractory or Cardiovascular Collapse):
- Bolus: 0.1 mg (0.1 mL of 1:1000 diluted in 10 mL = 1:10,000) slow IV
- Infusion: 1-10 mcg/min (titrate to effect)
- Requires cardiac monitoring
- Use for shock unresponsive to IM epinephrine

POSITIONING:
- Supine with legs elevated (if tolerated)
- If dyspneic: Semi-recumbent
- If vomiting/unconscious: Recovery position
- Do NOT have patient sit or stand (may precipitate cardiac arrest)

FLUID RESUSCITATION:
- Adults: 1-2 L NS rapid bolus
- Pediatric: 20 mL/kg NS bolus, repeat as needed
- For hypotension not responsive to epinephrine

ADJUNCT MEDICATIONS:

Antihistamines (Second-line, NOT substitute for epinephrine):
- H1 blocker: Diphenhydramine 25-50 mg IV/IM/PO
- H2 blocker: Famotidine 20 mg IV or Ranitidine 50 mg IV
- Helps with urticaria, pruritus
- Slower onset than epinephrine

Corticosteroids (Prevent biphasic reaction):
- Methylprednisolone 125 mg IV OR
- Hydrocortisone 200 mg IV OR
- Prednisone 50-60 mg PO
- NOT useful for acute symptoms
- May prevent late-phase symptoms (6-12 hours)

Bronchodilators (For bronchospasm):
- Albuterol 2.5-5 mg nebulized every 20 min or continuous
- Epinephrine is preferred if severe

Glucagon (For beta-blocker patients):
- 1-5 mg IV over 5 min, then infusion 5-15 mcg/min
- Beta-blockers may blunt epinephrine response
- Can cause nausea/vomiting

Vasopressors (Refractory shock):
- Norepinephrine: 0.1-0.3 mcg/kg/min
- Vasopressin: 0.01-0.04 units/min
- If epinephrine infusion inadequate

MONITORING AND DISPOSITION:

Observation Period:
- Minimum 4-6 hours after resolution
- 12-24 hours for severe reactions or risk factors for biphasic reaction

Biphasic Reaction Risk Factors:
- Severe initial reaction
- Delayed epinephrine administration
- Unknown trigger
- Previous biphasic reactions

Discharge Requirements:
- Epinephrine auto-injector prescription (2 devices)
- Allergy referral
- Anaphylaxis action plan
- Education on trigger avoidance
- Consider short course of prednisone (50 mg x3-5 days)

SPECIAL SITUATIONS:

Pregnant Patients:
- Epinephrine is safe and essential
- Same dosing as non-pregnant
- Left lateral decubitus position
- Fetal monitoring after stabilization

Beta-Blocker/ACE-I Patients:
- May have more severe/prolonged reactions
- Higher epinephrine doses may be needed
- Add glucagon early
- Aggressive fluid resuscitation

CONTRAST REACTIONS:

Mild (urticaria, pruritus):
- Diphenhydramine 50 mg IV/IM
- Observation

Moderate-Severe (bronchospasm, hypotension):
- Epinephrine IM
- Full anaphylaxis protocol

Pretreatment for High-Risk Patients:
- Prednisone 50 mg PO at 13h, 7h, and 1h before contrast
- Diphenhydramine 50 mg PO/IV 1h before
- Use low-osmolar or iso-osmolar contrast

EVIDENCE LEVEL: Level I (WAO, AAAAI, EAACI guidelines)

guidelines.title: Toxicology Antidote Dosing Guidelines
guidelines.content:
TOXICOLOGY ANTIDOTE DOSING GUIDELINES

ACETAMINOPHEN OVERDOSE:

N-Acetylcysteine (NAC):
- Oral (Mucomyst 20%):
  - Loading: 140 mg/kg
  - Maintenance: 70 mg/kg q4h x17 doses
  - Dilute 3:1 with juice/soda

- IV (Acetadote):
  - Bag 1: 150 mg/kg in 200 mL D5W over 1 hour
  - Bag 2: 50 mg/kg in 500 mL D5W over 4 hours
  - Bag 3: 100 mg/kg in 1000 mL D5W over 16 hours
  - Total: 21 hours

- Simplified IV protocol:
  - 100 mg/kg in 1000 mL over 2 hours (loading)
  - 200 mg/kg in 1000 mL over 10 hours (maintenance)

When to Treat:
- Acetaminophen level above Rumack-Matthew nomogram line
- Ingestion >150 mg/kg with unknown time
- AST/ALT elevation with APAP history
- Start NAC, don't wait for level if >8 hours post-ingestion

OPIOID OVERDOSE:

Naloxone (Narcan):
- IV/IM/SC: 0.4-2 mg, may repeat every 2-3 minutes
- Intranasal: 4 mg (one spray) per nostril, may repeat
- Titrate to adequate respiration (NOT full awakening)
- Opioid-dependent patients: Start 0.04 mg to avoid withdrawal

Infusion (long-acting opioids):
- 2/3 of effective bolus dose per hour
- Monitor for recurrent respiratory depression
- Fentanyl/methadone may require higher doses or longer infusions

BENZODIAZEPINE OVERDOSE:

Flumazenil (Romazicon):
- Initial: 0.2 mg IV over 30 seconds
- If inadequate: 0.3 mg IV at 1 minute
- Then 0.5 mg IV every minute to max 3-5 mg total
- Duration: 45-90 minutes (may need repeat dosing)

CONTRAINDICATIONS:
- Chronic benzodiazepine use (precipitates seizures)
- Co-ingestion with seizure-causing substances (TCAs)
- Elevated ICP
- Generally avoid unless iatrogenic oversedation

BETA-BLOCKER OVERDOSE:

Glucagon:
- Bolus: 3-10 mg IV (typically start 5 mg)
- Infusion: 2-5 mg/hour
- May cause nausea/vomiting - antiemetic before dosing

High-Dose Insulin/Euglycemia (HIE):
- Insulin: 1 unit/kg IV bolus, then 0.5-1 unit/kg/hour
- Dextrose: D25 or D50 bolus (25-50 g), then D10-D25 infusion
- Target glucose: 100-200 mg/dL
- Potassium supplementation often needed
- May take 15-45 min to see effect

Intralipid (Lipid Emulsion):
- 20% lipid emulsion
- Bolus: 1.5 mL/kg over 1 min, repeat q5min x2 if needed
- Infusion: 0.25 mL/kg/min
- For cardiovascular collapse

CALCIUM CHANNEL BLOCKER OVERDOSE:

Calcium:
- Calcium chloride 10%: 10-20 mL (1-2 g) IV over 5 min
- Calcium gluconate: 30-60 mL (3-6 g) IV
- May repeat every 10-20 min
- Monitor calcium levels
- May require continuous infusion

High-Dose Insulin: Same as beta-blocker protocol

DIGOXIN TOXICITY:

DigiFab (Digoxin Immune Fab):
- Dose based on digoxin load:
  - # vials = (serum dig ng/mL × weight kg) / 100
  - OR for life-threatening: 10-20 vials empirically
- Each vial binds 0.5 mg digoxin
- Infuse over 30 min (or bolus if cardiac arrest)
- Monitor potassium (hypokalemia may occur)

ORGANOPHOSPHATE/NERVE AGENT:

Atropine:
- Initial: 1-2 mg IV (severe: 2-4 mg)
- Double dose every 5 minutes until secretions dry
- May need massive doses (100+ mg)
- No maximum dose in organophosphate poisoning
- Endpoint: Dry secretions, NOT tachycardia

Pralidoxime (2-PAM):
- Loading: 1-2 g IV over 15-30 min (or 25-50 mg/kg in peds)
- Infusion: 500 mg/hour
- Reactivates cholinesterase (give early, within 24-48h)
- Repeat loading if no improvement

CYANIDE:

Hydroxocobalamin (Cyanokit):
- Adults: 5 g IV over 15 minutes
- May repeat 5 g if needed
- Turns urine/skin red
- Interferes with lab tests (CO-oximetry)

Sodium Thiosulfate:
- 12.5 g IV over 10-30 min
- Often given with hydroxocobalamin
- Or after amyl nitrite/sodium nitrite (older kit)

METHANOL/ETHYLENE GLYCOL:

Fomepizole (Antizol):
- Loading: 15 mg/kg IV
- Maintenance: 10 mg/kg q12h x4 doses
- Then 15 mg/kg q12h until level <20 and acidosis resolved
- Adjust for hemodialysis (give after each session)

Ethanol (Alternative):
- Loading: 0.6-0.8 g/kg IV (10% in D5W)
- Maintenance: 66-154 mg/kg/hour
- Target ethanol level: 100-150 mg/dL
- More side effects than fomepizole

IRON OVERDOSE:

Deferoxamine:
- IV infusion: 15 mg/kg/hour (max 6 g/day)
- IM: 90 mg/kg (max 1 g) if no IV access
- Continue until vin rosé urine clears and symptoms resolve
- Hypotension risk - don't exceed 15 mg/kg/hour

EVIDENCE LEVEL: Level I (AACT/ACMT guidelines, UpToDate)
